articles.title: Watching dolphins swim in the Strait of Hormuz
articles.description: Writing on Iran, Ukraine, Sudan, and conflicts around the world (Issue #427) Base image: Speculum Romanae Magnificentiae: Daican War by Antonio Lafreri, Nicolas Beatrizet For years, Medium writer Dylan Combellick has been doing in-depth daily updates on the war in Ukraine. Combellick, a retired analyst who lives on the ground in Ukraine, gives readers an extensive look at the various fronts of the war: The latest military actions; the reactions from the Russian home front; the latest in Washington; even news from other global conflicts. These updates on the war in Ukraine are some of the most-read, most applauded, and most shared stories on Medium. With ongoing conflicts in Iran, Ukraine, Sudan, and more, Medium writers have been sharing their perspectives on war. Let’s dig in. The atrocities playing out in the Middle East, the long-running war in Ukraine and the endless skirmishes across Africa and smaller developing countries create an unusual paradox: we are a highly cooperative yet violent species, though statistically, we are becoming less violent. — Shane Gehlert , Why Do War, Murder, and Honour Killing Persist in This Modern Age? On the ground One year ago, traveling student Abhinav Explores made a trip to Khasab, Oman . While most of Oman wraps around the edge of the Arabian Peninsula facing the Gulf of Oman and Arabian Sea, the city of Khasab is in a small enclave to the north, separate from the rest of the country. It lies directly on the Strait of Hormuz; you can see the coast of Iran from your hotel. But he wasn’t there for oil or geopolitics. Abhinav was there to see dolphins. Photo: Abhinav Explores “At the time, it felt like nothing more than a scenic stretch of water — full of dolphins and distant oil tankers gliding across the horizon,” he writes. “Looking back, I realize I was standing in one of the most strategically important — and fragile — places on Earth.” In his recent piece From Dolphins to Oil Tankers: My Visit to the Strait of Hormuz , he writes about his relaxed vacation to a rugged stretch of coastline that now has the eyes on the world of it. Looking back on his trip to see marine life, he writes, “[I]t feels more fragile than I realized — like a place balancing between movement, activity, and something much quieter that could easily be disturbed. With multiple wars constantly going on around the world — Iran, Ukraine, Sudan, and more — it’s easy to forget that these places aren’t just battlefields. They’re places to visit. They’re ecosystems. They’re homes. Kinihiko Iida recently turned 83. He was 3 years old when Hiroshima was bombed. For sixty years he never spoke about it. Most of his family, including his 4-year-old sister and 25-year-old mother survived the blast — only to die a few weeks later. The ease with which dozens of leaders around the world have once again begun threatening to use nuclear weapons has prompted him to speak out and break the taboo that for decades silenced the hibakusha [atomic bombing survivors]. — Sara Relli , How The Silent Survivors of the Atomic Bomb Have Turned Trauma Into a (Peaceful) Weapon Hormuz in the United States Back in March, high school teacher George Dillard noticed something interesting about his students — they did not want to talk about the then-recently launched war in Iran . A longtime teacher, he notes that his students “usually perk up at the threat of war.” In the days of the 2003 Invasion of Iraq, his students had forceful debates and talked about the conflict’s impact on their lives. When the United States assassinated Iranian Quds Force commander Qasem Soleimani in 2020, a student question and answer event about the conflict ran out of seats. This time? No interest. Specials sessions about the conflict were relatively empty; students seemed disinterested in classroom discussions. Dillard, a historian and prolific Medium writer, dug into the reasons in Why Don’t My Students Want to Talk About the War? He makes comparisons to previous wars, and how the war is being sold to the American people. But ultimately he comes to one conclusion. “It eventually became clear that they understood the significance of the war,” he writes, “but saw no point in trying to have a logical conversation about such an irrational event.” The shaving, the stripping, the public humiliation — it was designed for women’s bodies specifically. The men who had done the same or worse got to deal with it privately, if they had to deal with it at all. French businessmen who had grown wealthy selling goods to the German war machine faced fines or brief imprisonment, then went back to their lives. — Forward Press , When France Was Liberated, Men Shaved Women’s Heads in the Streets Fighting against silence It’s easy to compress wars into “poverty porn,” says Olivia Bussey in Whose wars are we allowed to forget? “Today, the images most people see of Sudan and of African crises more broadly are often limited to starving children, exhausted mothers, and long lines at food distribution points,” she writes. “[I]mages that are crafted to provoke guilt and pity, so we will donate, but that rarely invites us to understand the politics, the history, or the full humanity of the people shown.” But Bussey has first-hand experience with these conflicts: Her mother was born during Sudan’s first civil war in the 1950s and helped Sudanese refugees in Kenya during its second in the 1990s. Her mother’s experience with Sudan was “a country ‘blessed’ with fertile lands, rich soil and natural resources, where huge mangoes hung heavy from trees, sugarcane stretched tall, and the earth itself seemed generous” that nonetheless has been in some form of conflict since the middle of the 20th century. Today, a brutal armed conflict between rival military factions has torn the country apart. And yet, Bussey writes, the recent wars in Sudan have been met with cultural silence: “There are many nuanced and complex reasons why Sudan does not dominate headlines: geopolitics, competing crises, media fatigue, racism, and the way African conflicts are dismissed as endless and inevitable. But for some of us, silence is no longer an option. I write, I speak, I campaign not because it is fashionable, but because I am obligated by the force that was my mother. She told me plainly: don’t just sit and talk about it. Write about it, sing about it, pray about it. Let the world know that there was and still is a beautiful, fertile land, blessed beyond belief, that is being torn apart while too many look away.” For Vyacheslav, the meaning of life was finding a tow rope, convincing a sobbing woman to leave her home that had become [a] death trap, and saving a wounded grandfather. His van was nicknamed “Nezlamna” (“The Unbreakable”). He had been hunted by drones twice before. He had even shot them down with a shotgun. — A Brothers' Sister , Why the Death of a Ukrainian Volunteer Matters to Everyone Do not let silence be an option. Read more in the War tag on Medium to see the perspectives of people living through them. The CEO Signal: Semafor’s newest video podcast featuring the global CEOs shaping the moment we are in, hosted by Penny Pritzker and Andrew Edgecliffe-Johnson. In the latest episode, Verizon CEO Dan Schulman talks about leading Verizon back to growth and what leadership demands as AI reshapes strategy, operations, and the workforce. Watch or listen now. Deepen your understanding every week with the Medium Newsletter. Sign up here . Edited and produced by brandon echter , Carly Rose Gillis , and Scott Lamb . Like what you see in this newsletter but not already a Medium member? Read without limits or ads, fund great writers, and join a community that believes in human storytelling . Watching dolphins swim in the Strait of Hormuz was originally published in The Medium Blog on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

articles.title: 'Caloy' and 'Domeng': PH may see 2 cyclones this May — Pagasa
articles.description: LAPU-LAPU CITY, Cebu — One to two tropical cyclones may enter the Philippine Area of Responsibility (PAR) this May, the state weather bureau said. The Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration (Pagasa) reported that tropical cyclones during this month usually recurve and do not directly affect the local landmass. There are also weather systems that pass through the central parts of the country and recurve before exiting PAR. READ: LIST: Philippine typhoon names for 2026 This is based on data from climatological tropical cyclone tracks, weather specialist Leanne Loreto said during the Friday forecast, May 1. The bureau further [...]... Keep on reading: 'Caloy' and 'Domeng': PH may see 2 cyclones this May — Pagasa

articles.title: Dead gray whale at Moss Beach appears to have been killed by an orca, wildlife experts say

articles.title: Labor Day: Cebu to see fair weather with isolated rains, thunderstorms
articles.description: LAPU-LAPU CITY, Cebu — Cebuanos can expect generally fair weather with isolated rains or thunderstorms on Friday, May 1. This is according to the the state weather bureau today. The Visayas Pagasa Regional Services Division said the current weather would be caused by prevailing easterlies, which would also bring warm and humid conditions in the country. Cebu will also experience light to moderate winds and slight to moderate seas throughout the day. READ: Heat Index: Why Cebuanos should not take the heat for granted Similar conditions are forecast for the rest of the Visayas, Palawan, including Kalayaan Islands, and Occidental Mindoro. [...]... articles.title: Advocates in northwestern Ont., say citizen science can play a key role in managing blue-green algae
articles.description: Groups of dedicated researchers and volunteers in northwestern Ontario have been working to raise awareness about blue-green algae in freshwater systems and the risks that come with it. Now, they’re advocating for their work to be integrated into the reporting and monitoring systems already in place in the province.
